text: Later that session. Grubwin went to get water. Mawla is writing — genuinely writing, the kind of writing that means she's not really here anymore.
Vermia sits down beside you. Not in her usual burst-of-energy way. Just quietly, like a normal person.

speaker: Vermia
text: Your poem. From the exchange. The one you wrote about — whoever you wrote it about. I read all of them and yours had something.

speaker: Vermia
text: I don't mean technically. I mean it felt like it came from somewhere real. And I'm wondering — before you came here, were you a writer? Did you think of yourself that way?

speaker: Vermia
text: I wasn't either. Before the club. I just had feelings that were too big and words were the only containers that were the right size.

speaker: Vermia
text: I think you're the same. You have things that don't fit anywhere, so you put them in poems. And they almost fit. And that's all any of us are doing.
You don't have anything to say to that. She smiles — not the High Poetess smile, the other one — and goes back to her journal.
Mawla, still writing, says without looking up:

speaker: Mawla
text: She's annoying about it, but she's right.
